cue: Trace `top` as each post-increment write runs.
concept: top counts items = next free slot
prompt: What is the value of `top`?

cue: Count the writes, then step back one for the top slot.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Fill the array, then read the slot just below `top`.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Pre-decrement `top` first, then read that slot.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Compare reading at top-1 against pre-decrementing top.
concept: read a[top-1] and a[--top] hit the same slot
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Store at the current `top`, then advance it.
prompt: Complete `push(x)` on `Stack`: store `x` at index `top`, then advance `top` by one.

cue: Return the element one below `top`, leaving `top` alone.
prompt: Complete `peek()` on `Stack`: return the top element without changing `top`.

cue: Step `top` back, then return what sits there.
concept: pop steps top back then returns the slot
prompt: Complete `pop()` on `Stack`: step `top` back by one and return the element now at that index.

cue: Guard the empty case before stepping `top` back.
prompt: Complete `pop()` on `Stack` so that when the stack is empty (`top` is 0) it returns `undefined` instead of stepping `top` below zero.

cue: Reject when the buffer is full before writing; else write and advance.
prompt: Complete `push(x)` on `Stack`: if the buffer is full (`top` equals `cap`) return `false` without writing; otherwise store `x`, advance `top`, and return `true`.

cue: Read the last element by index.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Consider what Array.pop returns when there is nothing left to remove.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Push openers; on a closer, the stack top must be its match.
note: The stack height is the current nesting depth; a closer must match whatever opener is on top.
prompt: Write `valid(s)` that returns `true` if the brackets in `s` (`()`, `[]`, `{}`) are balanced and correctly nested, else `false`. Use an array as a stack.

cue: Keep a stack of unresolved indices; a larger value resolves them.
concept: next greater element via monotonic stack
note: Each index is pushed and popped at most once, so the whole scan is O(n) despite the inner while.
prompt: Write `nge(nums)` returning an array where position `i` holds the nearest later value greater than `nums[i]`, or `-1` if none. Keep a stack of pending indices and resolve each in one pass.

cue: Dequeue once, then measure tail minus head.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Divide by `cap` and keep the remainder; watch what happens as the value passes `cap`.
note: Modulo keeps an index inside a fixed buffer without any branch — the counter can climb forever while the slot stays in range.
prompt: What does this evaluate to?
snippet:
const cap = 4;
[3 % cap, 4 % cap, 5 % cap, 8 % cap];

cue: Wrap the write index with modulo capacity.
concept: circular write index wraps modulo capacity
prompt: What is the value of `writeIndex`?

cue: Write at the wrapped tail position, then grow `size`.
note: Wrapping the write index lets a fixed array reuse slots freed at the front, so no data ever shifts.
prompt: Complete `enqueue(x)` on `RingQueue`: write `x` at index `(head + size) % cap`, then increase `size` by one.

cue: Read at `head`, advance it modulo capacity, shrink `size`.
concept: ring buffer dequeue advances head modulo cap
prompt: Complete `dequeue()` on `RingQueue`: read the element at `head`, advance `head` to `(head + 1) % cap`, decrease `size` by one, and return the read element.

cue: Remove the front element and see what remains.
note: Array.shift is O(n): every remaining element slides down one index. A head pointer makes dequeue O(1).
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Read via a head index that post-increments each time.
concept: dequeue via a head index instead of shift
prompt: What does this evaluate to?

cue: Enqueue the start, mark seen at enqueue, drain with a head index.
concept: BFS visit order with a queue and seen set
prompt: Write `bfsOrder(adj, start)` returning the breadth-first visit order over adjacency list `adj` (array of neighbor arrays) from node `start`. Mark nodes seen at enqueue time. Use an array with a head index as the queue (no `shift`).

cue: Step the index back one, wrapping past zero.
concept: front push moves head backward modulo cap
prompt: What is the value of `head`?

cue: Back `head` up one slot with wrap, store there, grow `size`.
concept: deque front push steps head back
prompt: Complete `pushFront(x)` on `Deque`: move `head` back to `(head - 1 + cap) % cap`, store `x` there, and increase `size`.

cue: Shrink `size` first, then read the slot it now points at.
concept: deque back pop reads (head+size)%cap after shrinking
prompt: Complete `popBack()` on `Deque`: decrease `size` by one, then return the element at `(head + size) % cap`.

cue: Keep indices with decreasing values; drop the front when it leaves the window.
concept: sliding window maximum via monotonic deque
note: The deque front is always the window max; smaller trailing values can never win while a bigger, later one is present.
prompt: Write `maxwin(nums, k)` returning the maximum of every length-`k` window of `nums`, in one pass. Keep a deque of candidate indices whose values decrease front to back.
